event: Pollen and charcoal evidence suggests the earliest human activity on the islands, possibly by people cultivating barley before later Norse arrival.
Hiberno-Scottish monks (papar) are traditionally believed to have settled the islands and introduced sheep and oats.
Irish monk Dicuil describes islands north of Britain inhabited by hermits, likely a reference to the Faroes.
Norse settlers from Norway begin colonizing the Faroe Islands during the Viking expansion across the North Atlantic.
The Løgting, one of the world's oldest parliamentary assemblies, is established at Tinganes in Tórshavn.
Sigmundur Brestisson introduces Christianity to the Faroe Islands at the behest of the Norwegian king Olaf Tryggvason.
The Faroe Islands come under the formal control of the Kingdom of Norway.
A bishopric is established at Kirkjubøur, which becomes the religious and cultural center of the islands.
The Sheep Letter (Seyðabrævið), the oldest surviving Faroese legal document, codifies rules on sheep farming and land.
Through the union of Norway and Denmark, the Faroe Islands pass under the Danish crown along with Norway.
Construction of the unfinished Magnus Cathedral at Kirkjubøur reflects the medieval ecclesiastical importance of the islands.
The Protestant Reformation reaches the Faroe Islands, replacing Catholicism and ending the Kirkjubøur bishopric.
Magnus Heinason is granted trading rights and fortifies Tórshavn against pirate raids on the islands.
King Frederick III of Denmark grants the Faroe Islands as a fief to Christoffer Gabel, beginning the unpopular Gabel era of monopoly rule.
The Danish crown takes over the trade monopoly directly, controlling all commerce to and from the islands.
A catastrophic smallpox epidemic devastates the Faroese population.
The Treaty of Kiel separates Norway from Denmark, but the Faroe Islands remain under Danish rule along with Iceland and Greenland.
The Løgting is abolished as a legislative body and the islands are administered as an ordinary Danish county (amt).
V. U. Hammershaimb establishes a written orthography for the Faroese language, laying the foundation for a national literary culture.
The Danish trade monopoly is abolished, opening the Faroe Islands to free trade and spurring economic modernization.
The first decked fishing vessels are acquired, transforming the Faroese economy toward a deep-sea fishing industry.
A Christmas meeting in Tórshavn launches the modern Faroese national awakening, promoting language and cultural autonomy.
The first Faroese political parties are founded, including the Union Party and the Self-Government Party.
Faroese is permitted as a language of instruction in schools under certain conditions, advancing the national movement.
British forces occupy the Faroe Islands during World War II to forestall a German takeover after Denmark falls to Germany.
The Faroese flag, Merkið, is recognized by Britain for use on Faroese vessels during the war.
A referendum produces a narrow majority for full independence, but the result is annulled and the Løgting dissolved amid political deadlock.
The Home Rule Act grants the Faroe Islands autonomy as a self-governing community within the Kingdom of Denmark.
The Faroe Islands choose to stay outside the European Economic Community when Denmark joins, protecting their fishing waters.
The Faroe Islands declare a 200-nautical-mile exclusive fishing zone to safeguard their key economic resource.
A severe economic crisis driven by overfishing and bank collapse causes mass emigration and a Danish financial bailout.
The Vágar tunnel and later subsea tunnels begin transforming inter-island connectivity for the population.
The Faroese Home Rule government and Denmark sign agreements expanding Faroese authority over most domestic affairs and foreign relations.
Eight municipalities merge and infrastructure expands, including new subsea tunnels linking the central islands.
The Faroe Islands and the EU clash in the so-called mackerel war over fishing quotas in the North Atlantic.
The Eysturoy subsea tunnel opens, featuring the world's first undersea roundabout and easing travel around the islands.
The Sandoy tunnel opens, connecting Sandoy to the main road network and continuing major infrastructure expansion.
